questions.question_text: A broker meets a buyer at an open house and begins discussing the buyer's property needs. When must the broker provide a written agency disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: When beginning to work as the buyer's agent
distractors: When a purchase offer is submitted
Before the closing date
After showing the first property
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/15-35(a), a licensee acting as a designated agent must advise the consumer in writing no later than beginning to work as a designated agent on the consumer's behalf, in practice when the broker starts discussing the buyer's specific property needs, not at a later stage such as offer submission or closing. If the licensee is not acting as the buyer's agent, a written no-agency disclosure is required before confidential information is shared and no later than preparation of an offer.

questions.question_text: A broker begins working with a client as a designated agent. What must the required written agency disclosure include?
questions.correct_answer: The designated agency relationship and agent names
distractors: Commission rates and fee structures
The managing broker's personal background
A list of comparable property sales
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/15-35(a), the written disclosure must state that a designated agency relationship exists (unless a written agreement provides for a different relationship) and identify the designated agent or agents by name. It may be part of the brokerage agreement or a separate document. Since January 1, 2025, the licensee must also discuss the sponsoring broker's compensation policy, including amounts offered to cooperating brokers, but the written disclosure itself centers on the designated agency relationship and the agent's name.

questions.question_text: A buyer wants a non-exclusive agreement so she can work with multiple brokers. Under 2025 Illinois requirements, what form must this agreement take?
distractors: It can be oral if both parties agree
Only exclusive agreements require writing
A verbal commitment is sufficient
questions.explanation: As of 2025, Illinois requires ALL brokerage agreements to be in writing, including non-exclusive agreements. This change ensures that consumers and brokers have clear documentation of the terms of representation regardless of the type of arrangement.

questions.question_text: A broker starts marketing a seller's home based only on an oral understanding about the listing. Which statement is correct under Illinois law as of 2025?
questions.correct_answer: The terms must be in a written brokerage agreement
distractors: An oral listing fully satisfies the License Act
Writing is required only for exclusive listings
No agreement is needed until an offer is received
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/15-50(a) and the definition of 'brokerage agreement' as amended by Public Act 103-1039 (effective January 1, 2025), all brokerage agreements, exclusive or non-exclusive, must be in writing, and failing to have a written brokerage agreement with a client is grounds for discipline. The writing requirement is a compliance duty for the licensee: a designated agency relationship can still arise when a licensee works with a consumer, and a client may still seek to enforce an oral agreement, but the broker violates the Act by proceeding without a written agreement.

questions.question_text: A broker working with a buyer phones a for-sale-by-owner seller and asks detailed questions about the property's condition and the seller's flexibility on price. When must the broker give the seller a written no-agency disclosure?
questions.correct_answer: Before the seller reveals confidential information
distractors: Only if the parties meet in person
FSBO sellers are never owed disclosures
Only after the seller signs a listing agreement
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/15-35(c), a licensee must disclose in writing to a customer that the licensee is not acting as the customer's agent, at a time intended to prevent the customer from disclosing confidential information to the licensee, and in no event later than the preparation of an offer. Probing a FSBO seller about condition and price flexibility is exactly the kind of exchange the written disclosure must precede, whether it happens by phone or in person.

questions.question_text: A broker places a newspaper ad featuring only her own name and phone number, without mentioning her sponsoring broker. What type of violation is this?
distractors: A permissible personal promotion
A minor formatting issue only
An acceptable practice for print ads
questions.explanation: Illinois prohibits blind ads, which are advertisements that do not include the sponsoring broker's complete business name (225 ILCS 454/1-10, 10-30(b)). A sponsored licensee may not advertise under her own name alone: all advertising, in any medium, must be under the sponsoring broker's business name and the direct supervision of the sponsoring or designated managing broker.

questions.question_text: A broker creates a social media page to market listings. The broker's name appears in large bold text, while the sponsoring broker's business name is in small text at the bottom. Does this comply with Illinois advertising rules?
questions.correct_answer: No, the sponsoring broker's name must be equal
distractors: Yes, any mention of the name is sufficient
Yes, social media has no size rules at all
No, only the sponsoring broker may advertise
questions.explanation: Under 225 ILCS 454/10-30(f), when advertising includes a team name or an individual licensee's name, the sponsoring broker's business name must be at least equal in size or larger. Compliance can be shown by font size, by the area occupied by the name, or by a logo incorporating the sponsoring broker's name (68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.715(b)). A social media page with the broker's name in large bold text and the brokerage name in small text violates this prominence requirement.

questions.question_text: A real estate team wants to advertise under its team name on a website. Which additional requirement must the team meet under Illinois rules?
questions.correct_answer: The sponsoring broker's name must still appear
distractors: Team names are not permitted in ads
Each team member must be listed individually
The team must register separately with IDFPR
questions.explanation: Illinois permits teams to advertise under a team name, but the sponsoring broker's business name must still appear and must be at least equal in size or larger than the team name (225 ILCS 454/10-30(f)). Team names do not replace the sponsoring broker identification requirement, and a team is not a separate entity that registers with IDFPR; only a team name used in place of the brokerage's name must be registered by the sponsoring broker as an assumed name.

questions.question_text: A sponsoring broker's business is registered with IDFPR under the name 'Lakefront Realty Group.' A sponsored broker creates a flyer using 'Lakefront Realty' instead. Is this compliant?
questions.correct_answer: No, the exact registered name is required
distractors: Yes, similar abbreviations are acceptable
Yes, if the broker also adds a phone number
No, but this rule applies to digital ads only
questions.explanation: All advertising must include the sponsoring broker's complete business name: the name under which the brokerage is licensed or an assumed name registered and on file with IDFPR (225 ILCS 454/1-10, 10-30). Using a shortened or modified version of the name does not satisfy the requirement, even if it is similar, and the rule applies to all advertising formats.

questions.question_text: A broker launches a digital marketing campaign using paid search ads. Which Illinois advertising rule applies to this campaign?
questions.correct_answer: Sponsoring broker's name must be included
distractors: Digital ads are exempt from broker rules
Only print ads require broker identification
Paid ads need only a license number
questions.explanation: Illinois advertising rules apply to all media, including print, Internet, social media, digital forums, and paid search (225 ILCS 454/10-30(a); 68 Ill. Adm. Code 1450.720). There is no exemption for digital formats. The sponsoring broker's business name must appear in all advertising regardless of the platform, and unsolicited marketing and prospecting count as advertising.

questions.question_text: A managing broker asks how long transaction records must be retained under Illinois law. What is the correct retention period?
distractors: Three years
Seven years
Ten years
questions.explanation: Illinois requires managing brokers to retain transaction records for five years. This includes contracts, listing agreements, closing statements, trust account records, and correspondence related to transactions. The five-year period begins from the date of the transaction or the date the record was created.

questions.question_text: IDFPR sends notice that it intends to inspect a managing broker's transaction files. Can the managing broker refuse until a court order is obtained?
questions.correct_answer: No, IDFPR can inspect at any time
distractors: Yes, a court order is always required first
Yes, only with 30 days of advance notice
Yes, inspections always require broker consent
questions.explanation: IDFPR has the authority to inspect a managing broker's transaction records at any time without a court order. Managing brokers are expected to maintain records in an accessible manner and cooperate with IDFPR inspections as part of their regulatory obligations.

questions.question_text: A managing broker wants to store all transaction records electronically rather than keeping paper copies. Is this permitted in Illinois?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, if records meet IDFPR requirements
distractors: No, paper originals are always required
Only closing statements may be electronic
Yes, with no specific format standards
questions.explanation: Illinois permits electronic record storage, provided the records meet IDFPR's requirements for electronic document retention. Managing brokers must ensure electronic records are accessible, secure, and can be produced for inspection when requested by IDFPR.

questions.question_text: A buyer and seller both agree to sign a purchase contract electronically. Under Illinois law, what makes this electronic signature valid?
questions.correct_answer: Consent of all parties under UETA
distractors: Notarization of the electronic document
Approval from IDFPR before signing
Filing the document with the county clerk
questions.explanation: Illinois adopted the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act (UETA), which provides that electronic signatures are valid and enforceable when all parties consent to conduct the transaction electronically. No notarization, IDFPR approval, or county filing is needed to validate the electronic signature itself.

questions.question_text: Which of the following documents must a managing broker retain as part of the five-year record-keeping requirement?
questions.correct_answer: Trust account records and closing statements
distractors: Only the signed purchase contracts on file
Marketing materials and printed business cards
Personal tax returns of all sponsored brokers
questions.explanation: The five-year retention requirement covers a broad range of transaction documents including contracts, listing agreements, closing statements, trust account records, and correspondence. Marketing materials and personal tax returns are not part of the required transaction records that managing brokers must maintain.
